category_title: Students who interject on-topic comments to avoid reading text
student_description: This 9-year-old male with a language-learning disability masks his reading difficulties by making aggressive, on-topic comments about story pictures (e.g., 'I'll kill it') to avoid reading invitations. He needs to fulfill interactional demands without exposing his reading incompetence. The evidence reveals he uniquely requires a constructivist approach using short synopses, direct text-related questions, and physical gestures to safely scaffold his attention back to the words.

criteria.rationale: Allowing the student to choose his reading materials fosters ownership and increases his willingness to engage with the text over time.
criteria.check: The long-term plan must include a mechanism for the student to select his own books from a set of choices during shared reading activities.

criteria.rationale: Focusing on comprehension rather than isolated decoding allows the student to experience linguistic success and fulfill interactional demands without exposing his reading incompetence.
criteria.check: The long-term plan must prioritize meaning-making and comprehension discussions over rote decoding drills.

criteria.rationale: Establishing clear, consistent expectations for shared reading reduces anxiety and helps the student recognize his turn without feeling ambushed by reading invitations.
criteria.check: The long-term plan must establish a consistent routine or pedagogical expectation for shared and reciprocal reading turn-taking across multiple sessions.
